thm:1.summary: The landmark short-interval theorem: for any multiplicative $f:\mathbb{N}\to[-1,1]$ and $2\le h\le X$, the short-interval average $\frac1h\sum_{x\le n\le x+h}f(n)$ matches the long average up to $\delta + C'\frac{\log\log h}{\log h}$ for all but a small exceptional set of $x$ — so the two agree for almost all $x$ once $h\to\infty$.

thm:2.summary: Bilinear / multiplicative form at the $\sqrt{x}$ scale: $\frac{1}{h\sqrt{x}\log 2}\sum_{x\le n_1 n_2\le x+h\sqrt{x},\,\sqrt{x}\le n_1\le 2\sqrt{x}} f(n_1)f(n_2) = \big(\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\sum_{\sqrt{x}\le n\le 2\sqrt{x}}f(n)\big)^2 + O\big(\frac{\log\log h}{\log h}\big)$.

thm:3.summary: Short-interval theorem restricted to the factorization set $\mathcal{S}$ (integers with a prime factor in a prescribed range, $\eta\in(0,1/6)$): the mean-square version of Theorem 1 for $f\cdot 1_{\mathcal{S}}$, the form actually proved.

thm:4.summary: Bilinear theorem restricted to the factorization set $\mathcal{S}$: the $\mathcal{S}$-restricted analogue of Theorem 2 at the $\sqrt{x}$ scale.

prop:1.summary: The main proposition: for $F(s)=\sum_{X\le n\le 2X,\,n\in\mathcal{S}} f(n)n^{-s}$, $\int_{(\log X)^{1/15}}^T |F(1+it)|^2\,dt \ll (\frac{T}{X/Q_1}+1)(\cdots)$ — a Parseval bound on the Dirichlet polynomial obtained via the Halász inequalities and mean/large-value estimates; the analytic heart of the method.

lem:1.summary: The Halász mean-value functional $M(x,T_0)=\min_{|t_0|\le T_0}\sum_{p\le x}\frac{1-\Re f(p)p^{-it_0}}{p}$ controlling $|F(1+it)|$ — the pretentious-distance quantity that governs how large a multiplicative Dirichlet polynomial can be.

lem:2.summary: Lower bound for the pretentious distance $\mathbb{D}(f,p^{i\alpha};x)$ for $1\le|\alpha|\le x^A$: $f$ cannot simultaneously pretend to be $p^{i\alpha}$ across a wide range of $\alpha$, which localizes the relevant $t_0$.

lem:3.summary: Decomposition device: writes $R(s)=\sum_{X\le n\le 2X}\frac{f(n)}{n^s}\cdot\frac{1}{\#\{p\in[P,Q]:p\mid n\}}$, weighting each integer by its count of prime factors in $[P,Q]$ — the identity that exposes the factorization structure of $\mathcal{S}$.

lem:4.summary: Initial reduction: for $X/(\log X)^{1/5}\le y\le X$, the length-$y$ short-interval average already equals the long average up to a negligible error — reducing the theorem to genuinely short $y$.

lem:5.summary: Completely multiplicative model $g_{\mathcal{J}}$ for a subset $\mathcal{J}\subseteq\{1,\dots,J\}$ of factorization scales: replaces $f\cdot 1_{\mathcal{S}}$ by products that isolate one prime-factor scale at a time.

lem:6.summary: Mean value theorem for Dirichlet polynomials: $\int_{-T}^T |A(it)|^2\,dt = (T+O(N))\sum_{n\le N}|a_n|^2$ for $A(s)=\sum_{n\le N}a_n n^{-s}$ — the $L^2$ orthogonality baseline.

lem:7.summary: Large-value bound at well-spaced points: $\sum_{t\in\mathcal{T}}|A(it)|^2 \ll (T+N)\log 2N \sum_{n\le N}|a_n|^2$ for well-spaced $\mathcal{T}\subset[-T,T]$.

lem:8.summary: Large values of a prime-supported polynomial $P(s)=\sum_{P\le p\le 2P}\frac{a_p}{p^s}$ ($|a_p|\le 1$): bounds the number of well-spaced $t$ with $|P(1+it)|\ge V^{-1}$, the prime analogue of the large-value count.

lem:9.summary: Halász inequality for integers: $\sum_{t\in\mathcal{T}}|A(it)|^2 \ll (N + |\mathcal{T}|\sqrt{N}\cdots)\sum_{n\le N}|a_n|^2$ — the sharp large-value estimate driving the mean-square bound.

lem:10.summary: Duality principle: the bound $\sum_m|\sum_n a_n x_{mn}|^2 \le D\sum_n|a_n|^2$ is equivalent to its transpose, letting large-value problems be solved in whichever dual form is easier.

lem:11.summary: Halász inequality for primes: the prime-supported analogue of Lemma 9, the large-value input for the prime-Dirichlet-polynomial factor $P(1+it)$.

lem:12.summary: Factorization lemma: when $a_{mp}=b_m c_p$ for $p\nmid m$, $P\le p\le Q$, the weighted sum $Q_{v,H}$ factors as a product over the $m$- and $p$-variables — the algebraic input to the moment computation.

lem:13.summary: Moment computation: $\int_{-T}^T |Q(1+it)^\ell A(1+it)|^2\,dt \ll (\frac{T}{X}+2^\ell Y_1)(\ell+1)!^2$ with $\ell=\lceil\log Y_2/\log Y_1\rceil$ — bounds high moments of the factored polynomial.

lem:14.summary: Parseval bridge: relates the short-interval sums $S_j(x)=\sum_{x\le m\le x+h_j}a_m$ (for $1\le h_1\le h_2 = X/(\log X)^{1/5}$) to $\int|A(1+it)|^2$, converting short-interval variance into a Dirichlet-polynomial mean square.

cor:1.summary: Smooth numbers in short intervals: for $\varepsilon>0$, the number of $X^\varepsilon$-smooth integers in $[X, X+C(\varepsilon)\sqrt{X}]$ is $\ge \sqrt{X}(\log X)^{-4}$ for large $X$.

cor:2.summary: Nontrivial bound on Liouville correlations: for every $h\ge 1$ there is $\delta(h)>0$ with $\frac1X|\sum_{n\le X}\lambda(n)\lambda(n+h)| \le 1-\delta(h)$ — a step toward the Chowla conjecture, valid for any sign-changing completely multiplicative $f$.

cor:3.summary: Sign-change criterion: a real multiplicative $f$ has a positive proportion of sign changes iff $f(n)<0$ for some $n$ and $f(n)\ne 0$ for a positive proportion of $n$.

cor:4.summary: Sign changes in almost all short intervals: under the same nontriviality hypothesis, for any $\psi(x)\to\infty$, almost every $[x,x+\psi(x)]$ contains a sign change of $f$.

cor:5.summary: Sign change at the $\sqrt{x}$ scale: a sign-changing completely multiplicative $f$ changes sign in $[x, x+C\sqrt{x}]$ for all large $x$.

cor:6.summary: Smooth-number asymptotics in almost all short intervals: for $\psi(x)\to\infty$, almost all $[x,x+\psi(x)]$ contain $\sim \rho(u)\psi(x)$ many $x^{1/u}$-smooth integers, with $\rho$ the Dickman function.

edges.note: Section 9 derives the $\mathcal{S}$-restricted short-interval theorem from the main proposition's Parseval bound.
Section 10 derives the $\mathcal{S}$-restricted bilinear theorem from the main proposition.
The general short-interval theorem follows from the $\mathcal{S}$-restricted version via the factorization-set decomposition.
The general bilinear theorem follows from its $\mathcal{S}$-restricted version.
The initial reduction handles long $y\ge X/(\log X)^{1/5}$, leaving the genuinely short range.
The $\mathcal{S}$-restricted theorem is connected to the Dirichlet integral via the Parseval bridge (Lemma 14).
The Parseval bound rests on the Halász inequality for integers (Lemma 9).
The prime-factor part uses the Halász inequality for primes (Lemma 11).
The high-moment contribution is controlled by the moment computation (Lemma 13).
The factorization into completely multiplicative pieces (Lemma 5) underlies the proposition.
The decomposition device (Lemma 3) exposes the $[P,Q]$ prime-factor structure of $\mathcal{S}$.
The Halász inequality builds on the mean value theorem (Lemma 6).
The Halász inequality uses the large-value bound at well-spaced points (Lemma 7).
The estimate is set up via the duality principle (Lemma 10).
The prime Halász inequality uses the large values of prime-supported polynomials (Lemma 8).
The prime estimate also uses duality (Lemma 10).
The prime Halász bound is phrased via the mean-value functional $M(x,T_0)$ (Lemma 1).
Counting large values of $P(1+it)$ uses the mean value theorem (Lemma 6).
The prime large-value count uses the well-spaced large-value bound (Lemma 7).
The well-spaced large-value bound is derived from the mean value theorem (Lemma 6).
The moment computation uses the factorization lemma (Lemma 12).
The mean-value functional uses the pretentious-distance lower bound (Lemma 2) to localize $t_0$.
The Parseval bridge is an instance of the mean value theorem (Lemma 6).
Existence of smooth numbers in $[X,X+C\sqrt{X}]$ follows from the short-interval theorem applied to the smooth-number indicator.
Smooth-number asymptotics in almost all short intervals follow from the short-interval theorem.
The Liouville-correlation bound follows from the bilinear theorem at the $\sqrt{x}$ scale.
Sign changes in almost all short intervals follow from the short-interval theorem.
The $\sqrt{x}$-scale sign change follows from the bilinear theorem.
The positive-proportion sign-change criterion is deduced from the almost-all-intervals sign-change result.

main_theorems.description: The short-interval theorem and its bilinear $\sqrt{x}$-scale form.

restricted_versions.description: The forms restricted to the factorization set $\mathcal{S}$, which the analytic argument directly proves.

main_proposition.description: The Parseval / mean-and-large-value bound on the Dirichlet polynomial $F(1+it)$ over $\mathcal{S}$ — the analytic engine.

reduction_and_pretentiousness.description: The initial reduction to short $y$ and the Halász mean-value functional / pretentious-distance inputs.

decomposition.description: The Dirichlet-polynomial decomposition exposing the $[P,Q]$ prime-factor structure and the completely multiplicative models.

mean_and_large_values.description: The $L^2$ mean value theorem, the well-spaced large-value bounds, and the duality principle.

halasz_inequalities.description: The Halász large-value inequalities for integer- and prime-supported Dirichlet polynomials.

moment_and_parseval.description: The high-moment computation for the factored polynomial and the Parseval bridge to short-interval sums.

corollaries.description: Consequences: smooth numbers in short intervals, the Liouville/Chowla correlation bound, and sign-change results.

Halasz.title: G. Halász — mean values of multiplicative functions

Montgomery-LargeValues.title: H. L. Montgomery — mean and large value theorems for Dirichlet polynomials
